content.full_text:
May 5, 1991

Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Grossmont Union High School District

Dear Mr. Wallace,

Thank you for taking to time to do some research on the "Conflict
of Interest" issue that I raised today. The documents that I
have included are drawn from a Complaint that I mailed off today
to your Commission. Those pages and the Opinion of the 9th
Circuit Court should give you adequate information.

The Grossmont Union High School Board of Education has a contract
with a firm called Littler, Mendelson, Fastiff and Tichy to act
as general counsel to the district. (Copy enclosed) This contract is due to expire and revert to month to month June 30,
1991.

This firm has been the counsel to the district has represented
them during all of the litigation that my wife and I have had
with the district. For reasons completely independent of the
litigation, as a responsible board member, I feel that the district should consider looking for other counsel.

As it stands now, the district Board of Education has authorized
the legal firm to pursue the alleged violation of the contract to
the Supreme Court. The firm has offered to do it on a contingency. Should the district loose at the Supreme Court level, the
firm would not charge the district. Should the district prevail
and have the matter overturned, the district would pay.

The decision to pursue the matter was taken in closed session of
the board and without my being present.

In the normal process of things on our board, the Administration
of the district would recommend that the Board of the contract be
renewed, and this would take place sometime in late June or July.
I have an interest in getting it on the agenda sooner so that we
can look for other counsel if that is the decision of the board.

As a matter of interest, the district does not use the services
of the County Counsel. That relationship was severed in 1988.

The process of getting it on the Agenda is to request that it be
put on, and this takes two weeks. Our next meeting is May 9,
1991 and the following one is May 23, 1991. In order to get it
on the agenda for May 23, 1991, a request would need to be made
on May 9, 1991.

Question one for you is this. Is there any problem, i.e. a
Conflict of Interest, for me to request that this item be put on
the agenda for May 23, 1991? This, in a sense, is merely moving
ahead the district timeline by 6 weeks.

Since the decision has been made by the Board to continue the
lawsuit, and since there is precedence for the district hiring an
attorney independently to pursue matters already underway, is
there a conflict of interest (This is question 2) in my part in
participating in the discussion and the voting to determine the
provider of legal services for the district for the next year,
starting July 1, 1991.

Obviously I would disqualify myself in any discussion or vote on
whether to continue the suit or to hire or pay an attorney to
pursue it to the Supreme Court.

If I could get some answer on question 1, even by phone, prior to
the evening of May 9, 1991 it would be of help. If I could have
a written answer by May 23, 1991 I would appreciate it.

Sincerely,

Thomas P. Davies
P.O. Box 900009
San Diego, CA 92190

Business: (619) 287-7804
FAX:      (619) 287-6389

May 1, 1991


Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Complaint - Grossmont Union High School District

Dear Sirs,

     The Board Members of the Grossmont union High School Dis-
trict, to be later enumerated, have taken votes on matters, and
otherwise conducted themselves in a manner that I believe to be
contrary to the laws and procedures established by the State of
California and enforced by your office. I am sending this as an
official complaint and would like you to examine the issues and
make some determination. Following is a background and a state-
ment of the issues:

     On January 25, 1988, Thomas & Nadia Davies filed a lawsuit
in the Superior Court of the State of California against the
Grossmont Union High School District, its trustees and certain
administrators and staff members.

     Attorneys for the defendants were Littler, Mendelson, Fas-
tiff & Tichy. The attorney with primary responsibility for the
defense was C. Anne Hudson.

     On November 12, 1988, a civil compromise between the plain-
tiffs and defendants was reached. Said settlement provided a
paragraph containing the following language:

     ".... (Nadia nor Tom Davies) would never seek,
     apply for, or accept future employment,
     position, or office with the district in any
     capacity." (Appendix 1)

     The settlement also provided for the payment of $39,200.00
to the Davies.

     The Board members who were sitting and who were signatories
of the settlement were: Mrs. June Mott, Dr. Donald Hunsaker II,
Dr. Kenneth P. Whitcomb, Mr. Fred Andrews (Deceased - February 3,
1991), and Mrs. Betty Pengelley.

     On November 6, 1990, Dr. Thomas Davies was elected to the
District Governing Board of the Grossmont Union High School Dis-
trict.

     On November 15, 1991, at the final meeting of the old Board
of Education, the same five members, Mott, Hunsaker, Whitcomb,

Andrews and Pengelley authorized the same attorneys to pursue
enforcement of the settlement to set aside and/or reverse the
election of Dr. Davies. Two of the ousted trustees authorized
the attorneys to pursue their desire to set aside the election
results.

The reorganization meeting of the Board of Education would
take place in December where the newly elected board members
would be sworn in and new officers would be elected.

As a footnote, in the election held on November 6, 1990, Dr.
Donald Hunsaker II and Mrs. Betty Pengelley were ousted from
office, being number 5 and 6 out of a field of 6. Fred Andrews
replacement was selected by Dr. Davies, Mrs. Mott, and Ken Whitcomb on February 26, 1991. Dr. Maynard Olsen the other elected
candidate November 6, 1990, who was serving in Operation Desert
Storm returned to take his seat in late March. On April 4, 1991
the board was at full compliment.

On November 20, 1990, at the direction of the governing
board of the Grossmont Union High School District, Attorney C.
Anne Hudson advised Dr. Davies that his candidacy for election to
the board constituted a violation of the Federal court order. The
attorney also expressed the "District's" desire that Dr. Davies
reconsider any decision to accept district office as a governing
board member in violation of the settlement agreement.

Dr. Davies rejected the conclusion of the board and advised
the attorney that the general populace was not precluded from
selecting him to an elected position.

According to C. Anne Hudson's declaration dated November 30,
1990, she was "... given authority to settle only on the condition that the Davies would both agree that they would never
seek, apply for, or accept future employment, position, or office
with the district in any capacity." She had also explained to
her clients that "... a settlement could include Ms.
Davies' resignation and a promise from both of the Davies never
to apply for or hold a position of employment in the District or
to seek or hold an elected office in the District." (emphasis
added).

In their endeavor to remove Dr. Davies as a trustee, an
action to enforce the civil compromise and for an order to show
cause why Dr. Davies should not be held in contempt were filed in
the Federal court on December 12, 1990. In support of their
application, the trustees' attorneys claimed fees in excess of
$52,000.00. These fees are an obligation of the district incurred by the trustees (including those which have been ousted
from office) to remove Dr. Thomas Davies as an elected trustee of
the Grossmont Union High School District. Dr. Hunsaker, a trustee ousted by the voters, expressed his willingness to remain
as a trustee in the event Dr. Thomas Davies was unseated. (Appendix 2).

The hearing was held before the Honorable Lawrence Irving
who ordered Dr. Davies to be unseated under penalty of contempt
and ordered $25,875.00 as a reasonable sum for the school's
attorneys fees under all the circumstances in this case. This
amount was calculated on the basis of two-hundred twenty-five
(225) hours expended by the attorneys for Grossmont Union High
School District at the hourly rate of One-Hundred Fifteen Dollars
($115.00) which is the reasonable hourly rate currently charged
by attorneys to the Grossmont Union High School District.

Additionally, the attorneys said they began work on this
matter shortly after the November 6, 1990 election and continued
working on said case to the date of the last hearing before Judge
Irving on December 12, 1990. It is interesting to note that they
claimed two attorneys worked on this matter for a total cost
in excess of $2,000.00. Dr Davies' attorney fees throughout the
dispute were less than $7,000.00.

Immediately after Judge Irving's decision, Dr. Davies appealed the matter to the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals. This
appeal resulted in a published opinion on April 9, 1991. An
interlocutory opinion was issued on February 11, 1991 reversing
the District Court and vacating the contempt order and Dr.
Davies' resignation thereon. (Appendix 3)

The published opinion of the United States Court of Appeals
for the Ninth Circuit holds that,

"There is no question that the public
interest at stake in this case is of the
highest order. It involves the most
important political right in a democratic
system of government: the right of the
people to elect representative of their own
choosing to public office."

The Court of Appeal further held that the arguments of the
district for enforcement of the civil compromise were "pernicious". That although the defendant in this case is denominated
"the District", they could not overlook the fact that the real
parties in interest urging enforcement of the settlement agreement were the current members of the board.

Lastly, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeal in rejecting the
district's argument states as follows:

"To treat political rights as economic
commodities corrupts the political process.
Just as we would not enforce a contract
stating that voter X will vote for candidate
Y in exchange for a sum of money, so too we
will not enforce an agreement whereby a
citizen receives money in exchange for a
promise not to exercise his right to run for
office. As harmful as such agreements are in

general, they are particularly offensive
where, as here, the parties authorizing the
payments are elected officials and the
recipient is a potential political opponent.
This sort of arrangement is a serious abuse
of the power of incumbency."     (Emphasis
added.)

The import of the Federal Court's ruling is the abuse of the
current members of the Grossmont Union High School District's
board of trustees use of public funds to pay both Dr. Davies not
to run against them; to pay the attorney's fees of Littler,
Mendelson, Fastiff & Tichy in negotiating the underlying settle­
ment which was clearly unenforceable; and, to pay the attorneys
fees in pursuing the matter in the federal court.

     At this time, notwithstanding the published opinion of the
Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals, the district trustees have ex­
pressed their intent to pursue this matter to the Supreme Court
of the United States and to pay the law firm of Littler, Mendel­
son, Fastiff & Tichy in their endeavor. As a seated member of
the Board of Trustees, Dr. Davies has been precluded from any
discussions with regard to the incumbents intent.

     The decision to appeal was made on March 4, 1991 by Board
members Anthony Orosco, Dr. Kenneth Whitcomb and Mrs. June Mott.

     Someone, and clearly it cannot be the current members of the
Board of Trustees of the Grossmont Union High School District,
must look into the propriety and appropriateness of the actions
and expenditures being authorized by the incumbent board in their
request to unseat Dr. Thomas Davies, and their use of school monies
to do so. The charges for the district by their legal counsel to
defend their position at the appellate level are at this point
unclear. In my opinion there are several reasons why there was
an abuse of the position of the incumbents, and a conflict of
interest.

     First, in my opinion the 9th Circuit court of appeals has
provided adequate grounds in their clear comment on the abuse of
the position of the incumbents.

     Second, It is in the financial interest of the Board Members
to have included in the settlement the statement that they
believe precluded Dr. Davies from seeking office, and in seeking
enforcement of the settlement at the district and federal level.
Board members in the Grossmont District are provided $200.00
per meeting or approximately $400.00 per month. They also re­
ceive health and insurance benefits plus other reimbursed ex­
penses for authorized travel, meals and conference attendance.

     Dr. Whitcomb has been on the Board since 1971, Mrs. Pengeley
and Dr. Hunsaker were on the Board from about 1979, Mr. Andrews
from approximately 1984 and Mrs. Mott from 1986. In addition Dr.
Whitcomb is a Dentist in the La Mesa area of the Grossmont Union

High School District and specializes in Children's Dentistry.
You may recall that Dr. Whitcomb filed Bankruptcy and was a
subject of a complaint filed to the Fair Political Practices
Commission in May, 1990. Your findings were issued in October,
1990.

There is a third, and in my opinion, even more compelling
reason. It goes beyond the apparent desire to remain in office
and keep a potential contender at bay. It goes beyond any finan-
cial gain that they may have. It is simply that should the
district loose along the way, not being upheld, the district,
the law firm and the individual board members are potentially
exposed to litigation.

For example, the individual board members could be open to
a lawsuit by taxpayers upset over the waste of tax dollars and
possible malfeasance. It is also possible that they would have
to pay their own attorney's fees and the costs of any legal fees
that the district incurred. It is also a possibility that they
are open to malicious prosecution lawsuit and finally there is
the possibility that the law may allow Dr. Davies to claim from
them as individuals the costs of his legal defense, which has
been considerable.

Beyond all of this there is something curious and if it is
within your power, I would appreciate some clarification. When
the board met on March 4, 1991 to authorize their legal counsel
to proceed to the Supreme Court, Dr. Davies was specifically
excluded from the meeting on the grounds that it would be a
conflict of interest for him to be present. He even contacted
your office and spoke with Mr. Miamura prior to the board meeting
and was advised that he could not be present.

Why was Dr. Davies precluded from being present at the
meeting under the pretext that it was a conflict of interest for
him to be present, when the people present and making the deci-
sion had a possible conflict of interest in their meeting and
making the decision?

Since drafting the above, I received the material that you sent,
outlining the Conflict of Interest requirements and listing other
related laws. Rather than rewrite it all, I will try to be
specific in relation to some of my claims.

For determining Conflict of Interest, you outline four
requirements.

1. The official makes, participates in, or uses his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision.

The five board members, Hunsaker, Whitcomb, Mott, Pengelley,
and Andrews made the decision to approve the Settlement agreement
and to pay the $39,200 to the Davies in November 1988. They also
made the decision to pursue enforcement of the Settlement agree-
ment in November, 1990. Mott, Orosco, and Whitcomb made the

decision to appeal their loss to the Supreme Court in March,
1991.

2. It is foreseeable that the decision will affect the
official's economic interest.

Prior to the reelection of Whitcomb and Andrews to the Board
of Education in November, 1988 Dr. Davies had been supporting and
working with a group to recall Hunsaker and Pengolley. The
petitions were circulated and we gathered over 10,000 signatures.
There was a lot of negative information in the newspaper at that
time. Dr. Davies vigorously supported Fred Andrews and another
candidate. Dr. Davies opposed Ken Whitcomb and worked with a
committee that prepared a brochure that was circulated to an
estimated 70,000 voters homes. The brochure was very critical of
Board of Education practice, citing specific details. Dr. Whitcomb has a dental practice in the La Mesa area and practices
Children's Dentistry. I would presume his presence on the board
and his constant publicity would work advantageously for his
practice.

To oppose Dr. Davies or to keep him out of office would provide
some security to the incumbents. With regard to Hunsaker, he is
quoted in the paper as saying that he would be available to fill
the spot vacated by the resignation of Dr. Davies.

In court documents the district has made it clear that Dr. Davies
was troublesome and created a problem for the district. In
translation, it could mean that if Dr. Davies were not neutralized, and kept out of the field, it could mean political problems
for the incumbents - which it ultimately did. Dr. Davies was the
top vote-getter in the recent election.

Should the district not appeal the decision of the 9th
Circuit Court of Appeals decision to the Supreme Court, the
individual board members could be open to lawsuits as indicated
previously.

3. The effect of the decision on the official's economic
interest will be material.

For Dr. Whitcomb, keeping Dr. Davies out of office was very
important. Dr. Davies had filed a complaint with your office
before regarding Dr. Whitcomb. To put Davies on the board might
bring more negative publicity to Whitcomb and thus affect his
business. If Dr. Davies could not run, he could not oppose Dr.
Whitcomb or any other board member in an election.

Dr. Davies has filed with the Grossmont Union high school
district a claim for approximately $34,000 for reimbursement of
his legal fees. Should the district reject the claim, it is
Dr. Davies intention to file a lawsuit against the board members
individually for the recovery of his fees and any additional fees
that might be involved.

4. The effect of the decision on the official's economic

interest will be distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally.

This is self evident.

Sincerely,


Thomas P. Davies
P.O. Box 900009
San Diego, CA 92190

(619) 287-7804

Tuesday, April 9, 1991                    Daily Appellate Report                         3973

EDUCATION

Public Policy Void Settlement Term
Barring Any School Board Position

Cite as 91 Daily Journal D.A.R. 3973

UNITED STATES COURT OF APPEALS
FOR THE NINTH CIRCUIT

THOMAS DAVIES,
Plaintiff-Appellant,
v.
GROSSMONT UNION HIGH SCHOOL
DISTRICT; MONTE VISTA HIGH
SCHOOL; JENNIFER ELLIOTT; ROBERT
PYLE; PAUL MARTIN; CHRISTOPHER
BORIELLO; OREGON COMMISSIONS; and
DOES 1 through 20, inclusive,
Defendant-Appellees.

No. 91-55140
D.C. No.
88-0313-JLI(M)
OPINION

Appeal from the United States District Court
for the Southern District of California
at Lawrence Irving, District Judge.
and Gordon Thornton, Jr., District Judge, Presiding

Argued and Submitted
February 5, 1991—Pasadena, California

Filed April 8, 1991

Before: James R. Browning, Dorothy W. Nelson and
Stephen Reinhardt, Circuit Judges.

Opinion by Judge Reinhardt

COUNSEL

Kenneth S. Klein, James T. Hannink, Gray, Cary, Ames &
Frye, San Diego, California, for the plaintiff-appellant.

Richard J. Currier, C. Anne Hudson, Judith S. Islas, Littler,
Mendelson, Fastiff & Tichy, San Diego, California, for the
defendant-appellees.

OPINION

REINHARDT, Circuit Judge:

This appeal challenges the enforceability of a contract provision restricting appellant's right to run for public office. The provision is contained in a settlement agreement between appellant and the governmental entity involved. That agreement was formed the basis for a "stipulated and release and order thereon." We hold that the public policy favoring enforcement of such a provision is outweighed by the public policy served by its non-enforcement. We therefore declare the provision void as contrary to public policy and reverse the district court's contempt order based on its violation.

BACKGROUND

The appellant, Dr. Davies, seeks reversal of the district court's order finding him in contempt of a 1989 order dismissing his previous action against the defendant Grossmont Union High School District (the "District") in accordance with a settlement agreement, and ordering him to resign

immediately.

In 1988, Dr. Davies and his wife sued the District in state court, alleging violations of 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and various state law causes of action in connection with the District's transfer of Mrs. Davies, who had been employed as a teacher in the District. The District removed the action to the United States District Court for the Southern District of California, which remanded on the ground that state claims on discretionary grounds. Dr. Davies erroneously remained a named plaintiff in the federal action, although his only claim, for loss of consortium, had been remanded.

In November 1988, both Dr. Davies and Mrs. Davies executed an agreement with the District to settle the federal action. The agreement provided, inter alia, that the District would dismiss its actions in state court and would stipulate against the District in return for a payment of $39,200. The agreement also provided that "neither one nor both of the Davies[s] will ever seek, apply for, or accept future employment, position, or office with Defendant District in the future." The district court entered the settlement agreement and dismissed the federal action in an April 11, 1989 written order. The Davieses subsequently dismissed the state action.

Over a year later, on August 2, 1990, Dr. Davies publicly declared his candidacy for the Governing Board of the District, limited himself to the Board on November 6, 1990. After the election, but before he was sworn in to office, the District brought a motion to enforce the settlement agreement in the district court, seeking an order of contempt against Dr. Davies for violating the April 1989 order. While the motion was pending, Dr. Davies took the oath of office in another county. After a hearing on December 12, 1990, the district court granted the motion and found him in contempt of the 1989 order. The district court ordered him to resign his office immediately and to pay attorney fees to the District. The court denied his request for a stay of its order pending appeal to this court. Dr. Davies resigned, effective December 13, 1990. The record does not reflect whether he paid the attorney fees.

Dr. Davies appealed to this court and sought an emergency stay of the district court's order, or in the alternative, an injunction preventing the District from filling his position on the Board during the pendency of his appeal. A speedy resolution of the issues raised was essential to the Board, and to the Board, because the Board would be without someone other than Dr. Davies to the seat he had relinquished, Dr. Davies to the seat he had relinquished; and to long as the seat remained vacant, the Board could not make the crucial decisions necessary to the operation of the School District. Therefore, upon stipulation of the parties, on January 30, 1991, a motions panel of this court ordered that the case be placed on the next oral argument calendar. Further, in accordance with the parties' stipulation, temporary injunctive relief was granted. On February 11, 1991, we issued an unpublished order reversing the district court and vacating the contempt order and Dr. Davies' ensuing resignation. This opinion is issued in accordance with that order.

ANALYSIS

A. Jurisdiction

Dr. Davies asserts that the District may challenge his election only through the quo warranto proceedings afforded by state law. Cal.Civ. Pro. Code § 803. The district court had jurisdiction over this case, however, because district courts have power to enforce their own orders and to adjudge anyone in civil contempt who willfully violates such orders. See United States v. Grant, 852 F.2d 1203 (9th Cir. 1988).

APPENDIX 3

AGREEMENT FOR LEGAL SERVICES

(Including Participation In The

San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium)


This AGREEMENT is entered into by and between GROSSMONT

UNION HIGH SCHOOL DISTRICT (hereinafter referred to as the

"Public School Employer") and LITTLER, MENDELSON, FASTIFF &

TICHY, a Professional Corporation (hereinafter referred to as

"Attorneys").

The statutory authority for this AGREEMENT is contained

in California Education Code sections 35204 and 35205 and such

other provisions of law as may be applicable.

The legal services and fee schedule related to

participation in the San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium

are made available to the Public School Employer as the direct

result of discussions between Attorneys and the San Diego County

Superintendent of Schools. The hourly fee schedule for

additional legal services also was made available to the Public

School Employer as the direct result of discussions between

Attorneys and the San Diego County Superintendent of Schools.

The flat monthly fee related to participation in the San Diego

County Labor Relations Consortium has not been increased since

July 1, 1988, and shall not be increased until July 1, 1991.

By this AGREEMENT the Public School Employer

participates in the San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium

but is not automatically obligated to utilize Attorneys for

additional legal services.

1.

Participation In The San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium

A. Preventative Law. Attorneys will conduct at least six
seminars during the school year designed to update
participants with the latest developments in labor relations
and other matters.

B. Regular Newsletter. Attorneys will prepare and send a legal
newsletter on a regular basis covering labor relations and
other matters.

C. Employer-Employee Relations Advice. Attorneys will provide
individual advice on all employer-employee relations
matters. Attorneys shall provide such advice personally at
the offices of Attorneys or by telephone.

D. Negotiations Advice. Attorneys will provide individual
advice on proposals in negotiations and strategies and
tactics in negotiations. Attorneys will provide individual
advice on contract language. Attorneys will provide
individual advice on mediation and fact finding. Attorneys
shall provide such advice personally at the offices of
Attorneys or by telephone.

E. Personnel Administration Advice. Attorneys will provide
individual advice on all matters related to personnel
administration. Attorneys will provide such advice
personally at the offices of Attorneys or by telephone.

F. Written Legal Opinions. Attorneys will provide individual
written legal opinions at the request of the Public School
Employer on all matters referenced in paragraphs C, D and E.

2.

G.     The Public School Employer shall pay Attorneys a flat
monthly fee of $580.00.  The fee shall be payable for a
period of six months in advance.  The Public School Employer
shall have the right at any time, upon written notice, to
cancel this AGREEMENT.  If such a cancellation takes place
during a contract year, the fee will be prorated for that
portion of the year for which service has been provided or
for the actual service provided, whichever amount is
greater, provided that this amount does not exceed the
annual charge.

                              II

          Possible Additional Legal Services

A.     Attorneys agree to provide legal services to, and legal
representation for, the Public School Employer in regard to
matters including litigation not covered under the
participation in the San Diego County Labor Relations
Consortium.  Such possible additional legal services
includes actual representation in negotiations, grievance
arbitration matters, employee discipline proceedings, unfair
practice charges and employment discrimination charges.
Such possible additional legal services also includes advice
on general education matters not related to employer-
employee relations, negotiations or personnel
administration.

B.     The Public School Employer shall pay Attorneys at the rate
of $115.00 per hour for an available attorney, plus all
reasonable expenses, for any possible additional legal
services.

                              3.

MAY- 6-91 MON 17:46 IBERIA ENTERPRISES          2876334          P.15


                                    III

A.   It is expressly agreed and understood that Attorneys are

     independent contractors and not employees of the Public

     School Employer.

B.   It is expressly agreed and understood that either the

     Public School Employer or Attorneys may terminate this

     AGREEMENT at any time upon written notice to the other

     party.

C.   This AGREEMENT shall be effective on July 1, 1990 and shall

     continue until June 30, 1991, and from month-to-month

     thereafter.


PUBLIC SCHOOL EMPLOYER
GROSSMONT UNION HIGH SCHOOL DISTRICT

By: [signature]                    Dated: [illegible]

LITTLER, MENDELSON, FASTIFF & TICHY
A Professional Corporation

By: [signature]                    Dated: [illegible]
     Authorized Representative


1110ag.133

                                    4.

Since the decision has been made by the Board to continue the lawsuit, and since there is precedence for the district hiring an attorney independently to pursue matters already underway, is there a conflict of interest (This is question 2) in my part in participating in the discussion and the voting to determine the provider of legal services for the district for the next year, starting July 1, 1991.

Obviously I would disqualify myself in any discussion or vote on whether to continue the suit or to hire or pay an attorney to pursue it to the Supreme Court.

If I could get some answer on question 1, even by phone, prior to the evening of May 9, 1991 it would be of help. If I could have a written answer by May 23, 1991 I would appreciate it.
sections.facts:
The appellant, Dr. Davies, seeks reversal of the district court's order finding him in contempt of a 1989 order dismissing his previous action against the defendant Grossmont Union High School District (the "District") in accordance with a settlement agreement, and ordering him to resign immediately.

In 1988, Dr. Davies and his wife sued the District in state court, alleging violations of 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and various state law causes of action in connection with the District's transfer of Mrs. Davies, who had been employed as a teacher in the District. The District removed the action to the United States District Court for the Southern District of California, which remanded the pendent state claims on discretionary grounds. Dr. Davies erroneously remained a named plaintiff in the federal action, although his only claim, for loss of consortium, had been remanded.

In November 1988, both Dr. Davies and Mrs. Davies executed an agreement with the District, which served to settle the federal action. The agreement provided, inter alia, that the Davises would dismiss their actions in state court and federal court against the District in return for a payment of $39,200. The agreement also provided that "neither one nor both of [the Davises] will ever seek, apply for, or accept future employment, position, or office with Defendant District in any capacity." The district court approved the settlement agreement and dismissed the federal action in an April 11, 1989 written order. The Davises subsequently dismissed the state action.

Over a year later, on August 2, 1990, Dr. Davies publicly declared his candidacy for the Governing Board of the District; he was elected to the Board on November 6, 1990. After the election, but before he was sworn in to office, the District brought a motion to enforce the settlement agreement in the district court, seeking an order of contempt against Dr. Davies for violating the April 1989 order. While the motion was pending, Dr. Davies took the oath of office in another county. After a hearing on December 12, 1990, the district court granted the motion and found him in contempt of the 1989 order. The district court ordered him to resign his office immediately and to pay attorney fees to the District. The court denied his request for a stay of its order pending appeal to this court. Dr. Davies resigned, effective December 13, 1990. The record does not reflect whether he paid the attorney fees.

Dr. Davies appealed to this court and sought an emergency stay of the district court's order, or in the alternative, an injunction preventing the District from filling his position on the Board during the pendency of his appeal. A speedy resolution of the issues raised was essential to both parties: if the Board appointed someone other than Dr. Davies to the seat he had relinquished, Dr. Davies might have lost his ability to withdraw his resignation; and so long as the seat remained vacant, the Board could not make crucial decisions necessary to the operation of the School District. Therefore, upon stipulation of the parties, on January 30, 1991, a motions panel of this court ordered that the case be placed on the next oral argument calendar. Further, in accordance with the parties' stipulation, temporary injunctive relief was granted. On February 11, 1991, we issued an unpublished order reversing the district court and vacating the contempt order and Dr. Davies' ensuing resignation. This opinion is issued in accordance with that order.
sections.analysis:
A. Jurisdiction

Dr. Davies asserts that the District may challenge his election only through the quo warranto proceedings afforded by state law. See Cal. Civ. Pro. Code § 803. The district court had jurisdiction over this case, however, because district courts have power to enforce their own orders and to adjudicate anyone in civil contempt who wilfully violates such orders. See United States v. Grant, 852 F.2d 1203 (9th Cir. 1988).

AGREEMENT FOR LEGAL SERVICES

(Including Participation In The San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium)

This AGREEMENT is entered into by and between GROSSMONT UNION HIGH SCHOOL DISTRICT (hereinafter referred to as the "Public School Employer") and LITTLER, MENDELSON, FASTIFF & TICHY, a Professional Corporation (hereinafter referred to as "Attorneys").

The statutory authority for this AGREEMENT is contained in California Education Code sections 35204 and 35205 and such other provisions of law as may be applicable.

The legal services and fee schedule related to participation in the San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium are made available to the Public School Employer as the direct result of discussions between Attorneys and the San Diego County Superintendent of Schools. The hourly fee schedule for additional legal services also was made available to the Public School Employer as the direct result of discussions between Attorneys and the San Diego County Superintendent of Schools. The flat monthly fee related to participation in the San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium has not been increased since July 1, 1988, and shall not be increased until July 1, 1991.

By this AGREEMENT the Public School Employer participates in the San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium but is not automatically obligated to utilize Attorneys for additional legal services.

Participation In The San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium

A. Preventative Law. Attorneys will conduct at least six seminars during the school year designed to update participants with the latest developments in labor relations and other matters.

B. Regular Newsletter. Attorneys will prepare and send a legal newsletter on a regular basis covering labor relations and other matters.

C. Employer-Employee Relations Advice. Attorneys will provide individual advice on all employer-employee relations matters. Attorneys shall provide such advice personally at the offices of Attorneys or by telephone.

D. Negotiations Advice. Attorneys will provide individual advice on proposals in negotiations and strategies and tactics in negotiations. Attorneys will provide individual advice on contract language. Attorneys will provide individual advice on mediation and fact finding. Attorneys shall provide such advice personally at the offices of Attorneys or by telephone.

E. Personnel Administration Advice. Attorneys will provide individual advice on all matters related to personnel administration. Attorneys will provide such advice personally at the offices of Attorneys or by telephone.

F. Written Legal Opinions. Attorneys will provide individual written legal opinions at the request of the Public School Employer on all matters referenced in paragraphs C, D and E.

g. The Public School Employer shall pay Attorneys a flat monthly fee of $580.00. The fee shall be payable for a period of six months in advance. The Public School Employer shall have the right at any time, upon written notice, to cancel this AGREEMENT. If such a cancellation takes place during a contract year, the fee will be prorated for that portion of the year for which service has been provided or for the actual service provided, whichever amount is greater, provided that this amount does not exceed the annual charge.

II
Possible Additional Legal Services

A. Attorneys agree to provide legal services to, and legal representation for, the Public School Employer in regard to matters including litigation not covered under the participation in the San Diego County Labor Relations Consortium. Such possible additional legal services includes actual representation in negotiations, grievance arbitration matters, employee discipline proceedings, unfair practice charges and employment discrimination charges. Such possible additional legal services also includes advice on general education matters not related to employer-employee relations, negotiations or personnel administration.

B. The Public School Employer shall pay Attorneys at the rate of $115.00 per hour for an available attorney, plus all reasonable expenses, for any possible additional legal services.

III

A. It is expressly agreed and understood that Attorneys are independent contractors and not employees of the Public School Employer.

B. It is expressly agreed and understood that either the Public School Employer or Attorneys may terminate this AGREEMENT at any time upon written notice to the other party.

C. This AGREEMENT shall be effective on July 1, 1990 and shall continue until June 30, 1991, and from month-to-month thereafter.

PUBLIC SCHOOL EMPLOYER
GROSSMONT UNION HIGH SCHOOL DISTRICT

By: [signature]
Dated: July 5, 1990

LITTLER, MENDELSON, FASTIFF & TICHY
A Professional Corporation

By: [signature] Richard J. Curran
Authorized Representative
Dated: July 5, 1990

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: one for you is this. Is there any problem, i.e. a Conflict of Interest, for me to request that this item be put on the agenda for May 23, 1991? This, in a sense, is merely moving ahead the district timeline by 6 weeks.

Since the decision has been made by the Board to continue the lawsuit, and since there is precedence for the district hiring an attorney independently to pursue matters already underway, is there a conflict of interest (This is question 2) in my part in participating in the discussion and the voting to determine the provider of legal services for the district for the next year, starting July 1, 1991.

Obviously I would disqualify myself in any discussion or vote on whether to continue the suit or to hire or pay an attorney to pursue it to the Supreme Court.

If I could get some answer on question 1, even by phone, prior to the evening of May 9, 1991 it would be of help. If I could have a written answer by May 23, 1991 I would appreciate it.
embedding.first_500_words: May 5, 1991 Mr. John Wallace Fair Political Practices Commission 428 "J" Street Suite 600 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Grossmont Union High School District Dear Mr. Wallace, Thank you for taking the time to do some research on the "Conflict of Interest" issue that I raised today. The documents that I have included are drawn from a Complaint that I mailed off today to your Commission. Those pages and the Opinion of the 9th Circuit Court should give you adequate information. The Grossmont Union High School Board of Education has a contract with a firm called Littler, Mendelson, Fastiff and Tichy to act as general counsel to the district. (Copy enclosed) This contract is due to expire and revert to month to month June 30, 1991. This firm has been the counsel to the district has represented them during all of the litigation that my wife and I have had with the district. For reasons completely independent of the litigation, as a responsible board member, I feel that the district should consider looking for other counsel. As it stands now, the district Board of Education has authorized the legal firm to pursue the alleged violation of the contract to the Supreme Court. The firm has offered to do it on a contingency. Should the district lose at the Supreme Court level, the firm would not charge the district. Should the district prevail and have the matter overturned, the district would pay. The decision to pursue the matter was taken in closed session of the board and without my being present. In the normal process of things on our board, the Administration of the district would recommend to the Board that the contract be renewed, and this would take place sometime in late June or July. I have an interest in getting it on the agenda sooner so that we can look for other counsel if that is the decision of the board. As a matter of interest, the district does not use the services of the County Counsel. That relationship was severed in 1988. The process of getting it on the Agenda is to request that it be put on, and this takes two weeks. Our next meeting is May 9, 1991 and the following one is May 23, 1991. In order to get it on the agenda for May 23, 1991, a request would need to be made on May 9, 1991. Question one for you is this. Is there any problem, i.e. a Conflict of Interest, for me to request that this item be put on the agenda for May 23, 1991? This, in a sense, is merely moving ahead the district timeline by 6 weeks. Since the decision has been made by the Board to continue the lawsuit, and since there is precedence for the district hiring an attorney independently to pursue matters already underway, is there a conflict of interest (This is question 2) in my part in participating in the discussion and the voting to
